content:
AUDIENCE:  
This reader is a skeptical adult who distrusts sensational stories and is likely tired of exaggerated or clichéd narratives. They are pragmatic, value honesty, and prefer stories grounded in reality rather than hyperbole. They probably have experienced or witnessed tough job interviews and know that most are forgettable rather than horrendous. They care about authenticity and practical insights rather than dramatic flair. Overly emotional or overly embellished storytelling will bore or alienate them. A straightforward, slightly humorous but realistic tone with a touch of dry wit would resonate best.

===BEGIN===  
You think you’ve had bad interviews? Let me tell you about the worst one I ever had — and no, it wasn’t some Hollywood nightmare. It was startlingly mundane, which somehow made it worse. The interviewer was running late, so they shuffled me into a tiny, overheated room with no water and a flickering light. Then they spent half the time scrolling on their phone, barely looking up. When they did talk, it was to ask questions that had zero connection to the job I applied for, like my favorite TV show or what I had for breakfast. No feedback, no real conversation, just awkward silences and a ticking clock. I left feeling less like a candidate and more like a forgotten appointment. Not exactly dramatic, but honestly, that’s what made it so bad — a complete waste of time masked as a “professional” process.  
===END===
